title: Number of coral bleaching events by stage of the ENSO cycle
subtitle: [Coral bleaching](#dod:coral_bleaching) typically occurs when water temperatures rise above the normal range for the coral's habitat.  This is more likely during El Niño stages of the [ENSO cycle](#dod:enso_cycle) when tropical sea temperatures are warmer.
caption:
Number of coral bleaching events by stage of the ENSO cycle

Coral bleaching typically occurs when water temperatures rise above the normal range for the coral's habitat.  This is more likely during El Niño stages of the ENSO cycle when tropical sea temperatures are warmer.

This bar chart displays the annual count of coral bleaching events from 1980 to 2016, categorized by the stage of the El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO) cycle. The vertical axis measures the number of events, ranging from 0 to 80, while the horizontal axis marks specific years. A legend distinguishes three categories: El Niño events in dark red, Moderate stages in purple, and La Niña events in blue. The data shows significant variation in event frequency over the decades. The highest single peak occurs in 1998, where an El Niño year, the count reaches approximately 73 events. Other notable El Niño years include 2016, with roughly 54 events, and 1987, with about 26 events. La Niña years show lower frequencies generally, though 2010 stands out as a distinct outlier with a blue bar reaching approximately 53 events. Moderate years, represented by purple bars, cluster mostly in the lower to mid-range of the chart, typically between 5 and 25 events, with a peak around 40 events in 2005. Several years, such as 1981, 1985, and 2014, show very low counts near the bottom of the scale. The visual distribution indicates that the highest numbers of bleaching events coincide with specific El Niño years, while La Niña and Moderate years generally exhibit fewer events, barring the 2010 exception.
